problem_summary: 2024 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy type): teams act as a consultancy proposing a 5-year project to significantly reduce the illegal wildlife trade. Deliverables required (1) selecting a client organization with the authority, resources, and interest to run the project; (2) justifying the fit with literature and data-driven persuasion; (3) specifying additional authority/resources the client needs; (4) quantifying the measurable impact on illegal wildlife trade if implemented; and (5) assessing the likelihood of success via sensitivity analysis, plus a memo to the client. This Outstanding paper (Team 2426941) selects WWF and builds an integrated 4-submodel "SIWT" dynamical system to quantify impact across the whole trade chain (demand, poaching, trade, population).
models_used: Client Assessment (CA) model: a multi-criteria decision framework with 4 first-level criteria (Power, Resources, Interest, Influence) decomposed into 12 second-level indicators (10 benefit, 2 cost), scoring 8 candidate organizations
AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) for subjective indicator weights W1 — pairwise comparison matrix, eigenvalue/eigenvector solution, consistency check CI/CR (CR=0.068<0.1 with RI=1.54 for n=12)
Entropy Weight Method (EWM) for objective weights W2 — normalization, probability p_ij, entropy e_j, weight w_j=(1-e_j)/sum
Game-theory combination weighting: solves min(||W-W1||2 + ||W-W2||2) s.t. lambda1+lambda2=1 to fuse AHP and EWM weights into W*
Weighted TOPSIS: weighted distances D+ and D- to ideal/anti-ideal solutions, score S_i = D-/(D+ + D-); WWF won with composite score 0.78

OPN (Otential buyer-Purchaser-Non-purchaser) model: a compartmental SIRS-style ODE system over O/P/N populations with bilinear infection term beta*O*P/S and conversion/publicity rates (alpha, gamma, theta) to model demand-side dynamics

SPW (Stop Poaching Wildlife) model: a modified Lotka-Volterra predator-prey ODE system coupling poached wildlife W(t) (prey) and anti-poaching resources M(t) (predator), with a demand-coupling factor mu(t)=k*P(t)/S linking it to the OPN output

FIWT (Forecasting Illegal Wildlife Trade) model: discrete difference-equation recursion for monthly tradable volume X_t = R_{t-1} - I_t with carry-over remainder, a linearly declining purchase coefficient d_t = d0 - v*t, and a logistic-growth customs interdiction coefficient f_t; coupled to SPW via I_t = W(t)*C

FWP (Forecast Wildlife Populations) model: corrected exponential-growth population model A(t)=A0*(1+xi0+eta*t)^t - I(t) - K(t), with a time-varying growth rate xi(t)=xi0+eta*t and a legal-hunting offtake term K(t)
Parameter calibration by fitting all 13 SIWT parameters to historical data on elephants/rhinos (UNODC World WISE database), with a separate high-negative-impact (risk) scenario run

Sensitivity analysis on OPN (gamma) and SPW (r1) coefficients to demonstrate robustness; cost-estimation formula Pt(t)=M(t)*sum(fund)/sum(resource) to size the funding gap (~$6.51M for elephant/rhino protection)
key_techniques: Chain-of-models architecture: the four submodels are explicitly coupled in a directed pipeline (OPN demand -> SPW poaching via mu(t) -> FIWT trade via I_t=W*C -> FWP population via I(t)), so a single demand intervention propagates through the whole trade chain — this is the core innovation that lets one set of strategies produce coherent, quantified end-to-end impact

Repurposing epidemiology and ecology models as policy levers: SIRS for consumer demand and Lotka-Volterra for poacher-vs-enforcer dynamics, with each model parameter explicitly tied to a concrete policy strategy (e.g., publicity raises theta, enforcement raises tau1)
Hybrid subjective+objective weighting fused by game theory to neutralize the standard 'AHP is too subjective / EWM ignores expert judgment' critique — a credibility-boosting move judges reward
Logistic curve for the customs/government crackdown coefficient f_t, capturing realistic saturating enforcement capacity rather than linear growth
Counterfactual 'do nothing' baseline (population would fall 24.7%) plotted against the intervention scenario (+6.1%) to make the impact legible and persuasive

Explicit risk/uncertainty scenario: re-running SIWT under 'high negative impact' (bribery leakage, habitat degradation) giving a degraded-but-still-positive result (49.6% trade reduction, 6.9% population rise), demonstrating the dynamic-adjustment thesis
Concrete grounding: pinning the abstract model to real animals (elephants/rhinos = 70% of seizures per UNODC), real countries (India, China, Myanmar), and real Jan-2023 initial values (A0=41812)
Headline quantified results stated everywhere (52.4% trade reduction, 9.3% / 6.1% population increase, 41.2% demand drop, 46.64% poaching drop) — a results-forward narrative
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on completeness, coupling, and persuasion rather than on mathematical sophistication. It answers all five sub-questions head-on in order, never leaving a prompt requirement unaddressed (client choice, literature/data justification, extra authority/resources, measurable impact, likelihood + sensitivity, plus the memo). Its decisive differentiator is the integrated four-submodel SIWT system whose components are genuinely coupled — demand feeds poaching feeds trade feeds population — so the team can report a single, internally-consistent, end-to-end quantified impact (52.4% trade reduction, +9.3% population) tied directly to four named, actionable policy strategies. That mapping of every model parameter to a real intervention is exactly what Problem F (a policy problem) rewards: math in service of a decision. The hybrid AHP+EWM+game-theory weighting pre-empts the obvious 'your weights are arbitrary' objection, and TOPSIS produces a clean defensible ranking. The team also did the things judges look for to separate Finalist from Outstanding: a counterfactual baseline, an explicit risk scenario, a sensitivity analysis claiming robustness, real-data calibration on the dominant trade categories, and a well-written client memo. The narrative is relentlessly results-forward and persuasive, which suits a policy/consulting prompt.
section_highlights: The structure tightly mirrors the five sub-questions, which makes the paper easy for a judge to score against the rubric. Section 1.3 'Our Work' opens with a flowchart that maps each task to each model — orienting the reader immediately. The CA model (Sec 4) cleanly separates indicator construction, weighting (AHP+EWM+game theory), and ranking (TOPSIS). Section 5 'Why is WWF the Ideal Client?' is unusually strong for an MCM paper: it layers theory (SRI theory, stakeholder theory), literature support (Sas-Rolfes, Ayling, Mozer & Prost — real, citable sources), and data analysis (LPI decline) into a genuine persuasion argument rather than a model dump. Section 6 introduces the SIWT chain with a 'chain of illegal wildlife trade' figure, then for each submodel pairs the math with a named strategy subsection (Strategy I-IV), keeping modeling and policy visually adjacent. Section 8 grounds everything in elephants/rhinos with calibrated parameter tables (Tables 10-13) and an algorithm box (Table 14) for the FIWT difference equation. Results are always quantified and accompanied by 'do nothing' counterfactuals. The closing memo restates the headline numbers and the four-strategy framework in client-facing language. Notation table, assumptions-with-justifications, and a strengths/weaknesses section are all present as expected.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Parameter overfitting / opacity: 13 SIWT parameters were hand-fitted to historical data with no stated fitting method, error metric, goodness-of-fit, or confidence intervals — the calibration is asserted, not demonstrated, and the AHP pairwise matrix is omitted ('not shown due to space')

Internal inconsistency in the headline number: the population increase is reported as 9.3% in the summary/Sec 8.3 but as 6.1% in Sec 8.2 and the memo — an uncaught contradiction that a stricter judge would flag

Thin sensitivity analysis: only two parameters (gamma in OPN, r1 in SPW) are varied, only on the two 'upstream' submodels, with conclusions ('does not change much') stated qualitatively rather than quantified; robustness is claimed more than shown. The Fig 17 data also looks garbled

Questionable population dynamics: A(t)=A0*(1+xi0+eta*t)^t makes the base grow super-exponentially as eta*t compounds inside a t-power, which is likely not the intended demographic behavior and is not validated

Persuasion occasionally outruns rigor: the OPN 'attraction' term is borrowed wholesale from disease transmission without justifying that wildlife-product demand actually spreads via buyer-to-buyer contact; the k scale factor and the legal-hunting offtake K(t) are introduced with convenient proportionality assumptions

Data provenance is weak: many initial values are admitted to be estimated from government reports with possible error (acknowledged in Weaknesses), and 'missing value filling' for the 12 indicators is unspecified
Numerous typos and rough English ('Otential', 'Strantge', 'tosopsis', mislabeled figures e.g. two 'Figure 12'/'OPN model results' captions) — tolerated here but a real risk at the margin
An AI Report appendix is included documenting LLM use for brainstorming client options and strategies — required transparency, but the strategies closely track the LLM output
reusable_patterns: The 'coupled chain-of-submodels' template for any system/policy problem: decompose the target process into stages, build one small dynamical model per stage, and explicitly pass each model's output as the driving input to the next so a single intervention yields one consistent end-to-end metric. This is the single most transferable idea in the paper

Standard MCDM client/option-selection stack: 4 criteria -> ~12 indicators (mark benefit/cost) -> AHP weights + EWM weights -> game-theory fusion -> weighted TOPSIS score. Directly reusable for any 'choose the best X' sub-question, common in ICM policy problems

Map every model parameter to a concrete, named intervention (Strategy I-IV) so the modeling section doubles as the policy recommendation — essential for policy-type prompts where the deliverable is a decision
Always plot a 'do nothing' counterfactual alongside the intervention scenario to make impact legible and persuasive
Run an explicit degraded 'high-negative-impact / risk' scenario in addition to the base case to show the conclusion is robust to bad conditions, then argue dynamic adjustment recovers the goal
Use a logistic curve for any capability that saturates (enforcement, adoption, awareness) rather than linear growth

Borrow a well-known mechanistic model (SIR/SIRS for demand spread, Lotka-Volterra for adversarial actor pairs like poachers-vs-enforcers, exponential-with-offtake for populations) instead of generic regression — it gives interpretable parameters that map to levers

Section-per-sub-question structure plus a task->model flowchart in the intro, a notation table, assumptions-with-justifications, a strengths/weaknesses section, and a client-facing memo that restates headline numbers — a reliable scaffold for ICM policy papers

Ground an abstract model in the dominant real-world slice (here, elephants/rhinos = 70% of seizures) and specific actors/countries with dated initial values, to convert a generic model into a credible case study
